coredata.dc:title: Li-ion battery design through microstructural optimization using generative AI
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Kench, Steve
Squires, Isaac
Dahari, Amir
Brosa Planella, Ferran
Roberts, Scott A.
Cooper, Samuel J.
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries are used across various applications, necessitating tailored cell designs to enhance performance. Optimizing electrode manufacturing parameters is a key route to achieving this, as these parameters directly influence the microstructure and performance of the cells. However, linking process parameters to performance is complex, and experimental or modeling campaigns are often slow and expensive. This study introduces a fast computational optimization framework for electrode manufacturing parameters. A generative model, trained on a small dataset of microstructural images associated with different manufacturing parameters, efficiently generates representative microstructures for new parameters. This model is integrated into a Bayesian optimization loop that includes microstructure generation, characterization, and simulation, aiming to find optimal manufacturing parameters for a particular application. Significant improvement in the energy density of a 4680 cell is achieved through bespoke cell design, highlighting the importance of cell-scale normalization. The framework’s modularity allows its application to various advanced materials manufacturing scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: microstructure
batteries
electrode design
machine learning
generative AI

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by funding from the EPSRC Faraday Institution Multi-Scale Modeling project ( https://faraday.ac.uk/ ; EP/S003053/1, grant number FIRG003 received by S.K.). The Titan Xp GPU used for this research was kindly donated by the Nvidia Corporation through their GPU Grant program to S.J.C. The authors would also like to thank the tldr group for their valuable comments. This paper describes objective technical results and analysis. Any subjective views or opinions that might be expressed in the paper do not necessarily represent the views of the United States (US) Department of Energy or the US government. This article has been authored by an employee of National Technology & Engineering Solutions of Sandia, LLC, under contract no. DE-NA0003525 , with the US Department of Energy ( DOE ). The employee owns all right, title, and interest in and to the article and is solely responsible for its contents. The US government retains and the publisher, by accepting the article for publication, acknowledges that the US government retains a non-exclusive, paid-up, irrevocable, worldwide license to publish or reproduce the published form of this article or allow others to do so, for US government purposes. The DOE will provide public access to these results of federally sponsored research in accordance with the DOE Public Access Plan ( https://www.energy.gov/downloads/doe-public-access-plan ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bayesian optimization loop

Microstructure generation involves the prediction of a representative 2563 voxel cube microstructure based on a manufacturing label vector, y, using a trained generative model. Characterization of this dataset using TauFactor 2 allows key microstructural properties to be extracted. A discharge simulation then uses these properties to parameterize a P2D model, which returns performance at the cell-level. Finally, an acquisition function determines the best next label to test. In the example shown here, active material wt % and porosity are optimized.
Optimization results showing the specific energy for tested parameters

The color of each dot represents the specific energy at the cell level for a microstructure generated with a given parameter set. In the five plots showing individual optimizations at a given cell-level power, the black circled dot indicates the optimal parameter set that gives the highest specific energy. In the final subplot, these black circled points are plotted together to show the path of optimality through the parameter space.
Electrode optimization results for 4 parameters
(A) Ragone plot showing best achievable energy density at a given power and the high-energy and high-power design cases.

(B) Bar plot showing volume (left axis) and mass (right axis) of cell normalized components for the energy and power cells. Note that electrolyte mass is included in calculation for the mass and volume of each of the anode, cathode, and separators according to their porosities, which are assumed to be filled with electrolyte.

(C) 3D render of the high-energy and high-power cells. The cathode volume is generated by the generative model with optimized parameters, with an appropriate thickness anode chosen by capacity matching. Note that the volumes shown are for illustration purposes and would not be large enough to be considered representative.
Comparison of training data and generated microstructures for different manufacturing parameter sets
Increasing active material weight percent results in an increase in active material (black phase) and a decrease in binder (gray phase). Increasing porosity results in more white phase (pore).
Convergence analysis for the tortuosity factor
(A) The distribution of tortuosities for 200 samples from a trained generative model with increasing volume size.
(B) The nominal mean error against the true tortuosity factor (which is calculated from 50 volumes of size 800 voxel edge length).
(C) The reducing standard deviation with volume size.

(D) The required number of samples for a confidence of 95% that an extracted mean will have an error of less than 1%. Note that this error is for the mean the tortuosity factor of that particular cube size, not the true mean for a large volume, as small cubes converge to a different tortuosity factor values due to the influence of boundary conditions. This graph should thus be used alongside (B) to determine how accurate the mean is. Further information on this calculation is given in supplemental experimental procedures section A.
(E) The mean time to solve the tortuosity factor per sample.

(F) The total time to solve the required number of samples given in (D). Note that the decrease in time required at 384 compared to 352 voxel edge length is due to the number of samples required going from 3 to 2, which outweighs the increase in solve time for the larger volume.
Cell normalization procedure, with standard 4680 cell dimensions
Data taken from a cell tear-down study. 29

(A) An example electrode, where the “
∼
” signifies dimensions that can vary during optimization.
(B) Jelly roll dimensions, with the length of the jelly roll determined by the thickness of the electrode.
(C) Cylindrical 4680 cell, showing the fitted jelly roll, tabs at the top and bottom of the roll, and the casing.
Comparison of two 4680 cells (ca. 0.133 L volume) optimized for 10 W (energy cell) and 200 W (power cell) with their manufacturing parameters and performance metrics

row.entry: Energy density at 10 W discharge/Wh L1
Energy density at 200 W discharge/Wh L1
Specific energy at 10 W discharge/Wh kg1
Specific energy at 200 W discharge/Wh kg1

head.ce:title: Li-ion battery design through microstructural optimization using generative AI

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dyson School of Design Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2DB, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dyson School of Design Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2DB, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Faraday Institution, Harwell Campus, Didcot OX11 0RA, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Faraday Institution, Harwell Campus, Didcot OX11 0RA, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mathematics Institute, University of Warwick, Gibbet Hill Road, Coventry CV4 7AL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mathematics Institute, University of Warwick, Gibbet Hill Road, Coventry CV4 7AL, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thermal/Fluid Component Sciences Department, Sandia National Laboratories, Albuquerque, NM 87185, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Thermal/Fluid Component Sciences Department, Sandia National Laboratories, Albuquerque, New Mexico 87185, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries are used across various applications, necessitating tailored cell designs to enhance performance. Optimizing electrode manufacturing parameters is a key route to achieving this, as these parameters directly influence the microstructure and performance of the cells. However, linking process parameters to performance is complex, and experimental or modeling campaigns are often slow and expensive. This study introduces a fast computational optimization framework for electrode manufacturing parameters. A generative model, trained on a small dataset of microstructural images associated with different manufacturing parameters, efficiently generates representative microstructures for new parameters. This model is integrated into a Bayesian optimization loop that includes microstructure generation, characterization, and simulation, aiming to find optimal manufacturing parameters for a particular application. Significant improvement in the energy density of a 4680 cell is achieved through bespoke cell design, highlighting the importance of cell-scale normalization. The framework’s modularity allows its application to various advanced materials manufacturing scenarios.

The microstructure of lithium-ion battery electrodes strongly affects the cell-level performance. Our study presents a computational design workflow that employs a generative AI from Polaron to rapidly predict optimal manufacturing parameters for battery electrodes. After training a generative model on a small dataset of two-dimensional (2D) microstructural images, we can efficiently generate representative microstructures for new parameters, enabling us to integrate this capability into a Bayesian optimization loop. This allows for the identification of optimal manufacturing conditions that enhance performance, such as energy density.

Improved battery performance can accelerate the adoption of electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems, contributing to reduced carbon emissions and a sustainable energy future. Our framework’s modularity also makes it applicable to a broad range of advanced materials, potentially transforming how industries approach material design and manufacturing.

In this study, we introduce a computational framework using generative AI to optimize lithium-ion battery electrode design. By rapidly predicting ideal manufacturing conditions, our method enhances battery performance and efficiency. This advancement can significantly impact electric vehicle technology and large-scale energy storage, contributing to a sustainable future. The approach’s versatility also opens new possibilities for various advanced materials, revolutionizing how industries design and manufacture high-performance components.

ce:section.ce:para:
As we transition to net-zero carbon emissions, Li-ion batteries (LiBs) are increasingly relied on for myriad applications, including electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage. 1
,
2
,
3 Crucially, from one use case to the next, the relative importance of key cell performance metrics, such as energy density, lifetime, and cost, can vary significantly. Furthermore, consumer needs (e.g., expected electric vehicle range) and market forces (e.g., raw material costs) are constantly evolving. 4
,
5 This dynamic and diverse market means that the ability to rapidly optimize a cell for a specific scenario is highly desirable. 6 Microstructural design is a particularly appealing route to achieve this goal for two key reasons. First, an electrode’s behavior is sensitive to microstructural properties; for example, tortuosity factor and active material (AM) volume fraction strongly influence accessible energy at a given power. 7
,
8
,
9 This yields a wide space of attainable performances, which suits the range of desired applications. Second, microstructure can be tuned through the adjustment of common manufacturing parameters such as calendering pressure and particle size distribution. 10
,
11 It is thus feasible to test and produce different electrodes without altering established manufacturing lines or developing new chemistries.

Microstructural design requires a deep understanding of the complex manufacturing parameter/microstructure relationships inherent to LiB electrodes. To date, some progress has been made through the development of multi-physics simulations of electrode production. 10
,
11
,
12
,
13 However, while numerical methods can be far faster than the equivalent experimental campaign, they still require large experimental datasets for parameterization and validation. In addition, due to the complexity of manufacturing processes, simulations typically simplify the processes in order to become numerically tractable (e.g., by assuming spherical particles). Furthermore, as shown in this work, numerous large volumes must often be simulated to ensure high statistical confidence in a microstructure’s behavior. This can be prohibitively time and resource consuming in the context of LiB manufacturing optimization, where hundreds of evaluations may be necessary to determine the best processing parameters for peak performance.

In this study, we use a data-driven generative AI approach to learn the relationships between manufacturing parameters and electrode microstructure. Generative AI methods have previously been used in the field of materials science to learn to approximate the distribution of features in a microstructural dataset and generate realistic instances. 14
,
15 However, this approach has limited scope for optimization, as these models can typically only generate features associated with a single material made using a particular manufacturing condition. Other design problems, such as airplane wing profile optimization, 16 have been successfully tackled using generative AI. This method allows a generative model to produce a wide variety of outputs, drawing on features from a diverse training set. In this example, once an optimal wing profile is determined, realizing the new design is achieved by simply passing the desired dimensions to computer-numerical-controlled manufacturing techniques. In contrast, the relationship between the microstructure of porous composites (such as battery electrodes) and their manufacturing parameters is highly complex; thus, if these techniques were employed for microstructural optimization, then an excellent microstructure might be determined but with no knowledge of how to manufacture it.

Here, we use a model developed by Polaron 17 that is able to produce multiple image types associated with different manufacturing parameters. The data-driven nature of this approach avoids the need for computationally expensive physics-based simulations by directly learning complex features from training images. Importantly, provided there are sufficient training data, materials associated with manufacturing parameters not included in the training set can be predicted, as long as they are inside the envelope of the training data. This means that once trained, the model can be nested in a Bayesian optimization loop to determine the best microstructure, as shown in Figure 1 . Each iteration, the pre-trained model generates a batch of microstructures associated with given manufacturing parameters. Key microstructural metrics are then calculated from the generated dataset using TauFactor 2
18 and passed to a cell-level simulation called PyBaMM. 19 The simulation returns, for example, accessible cell capacity, offering fast microstructurally informed performance analysis. An acquisition function then determines the best manufacturing parameters to query for the next iteration, and the process is repeated. This builds a performance landscape covering the space of manufacturable cell designs, ultimately yielding manufacturing parameters that give optimal performance.

Although it is, in principle, possible to relate manufacturing parameters directly to cell performance, the addition of a microstructure as an intermediary requires the model to also predict an information-dense (and industrially common) image-based data type. In this sense, the trained model can be thought of as acting as a fuzzy constraint on the space of possible electrodes.

To demonstrate the optimization approach described in this work, we train the generative model using a dataset from Srivastava et al. at Sandia National Laboratories 20 (although real imaging data would have been preferred, were it available in the literature). These data were generated using a discrete element physics-based manufacturing model. Three common parameters are varied to create a dataset of microstructures. First is the AM weight percent (AM%), which is the ratio of AM to carbon binder domain in the slurry. This was varied in the range 92–96 AM%. Second is porosity, which is varied from 30% to 50%. Porosity is not itself a direct processing parameter but can be closely controlled through calendering. We would have preferred to use calendering pressure in this study, but no data of sufficient quality were available in the literature. Finally, there is a binder adhesion parameter, which controls whether the binder adheres more favorably to AM (resulting in a thin coating of binder over AM particles) or to itself (resulting in a clumps of binder). Srivastava et al. define an arbitrary range for the binder adhesion parameter, which is normalized to between 0 and 1 in this work. 20 The training set consists of representative cross-sectional images from the extremities of our chosen parameter space cube, namely the 8 corner points, 12 edge mid-points, and the center point (21 images in total). Training the generative model on this dataset takes less than a day on an NVIDIA RTX6000 GPU with 48 GB memory.

Alongside this optimization framework, we present two key findings for cell design. First, we show the importance of cell-level normalization and highlight that if an electrode’s capacity is optimized with respect to the mass of AM, then the results will not resemble an industrially relevant electrode. Second, we demonstrate the importance of representativeness and provide a method for quantitatively determining the minimum size and number of microstructures required to reach a given confidence in calculated metrics.

The results from three optimization scenarios are explored in this section. In each case, a number of experimental design decisions are made. First, we must choose which parameters to optimize, assuming all others are held constant. In this paper, we can select from 4 manufacturing parameters: AM%, porosity, binder adhesion, and mass loading. Next, we choose the condition under which we test the cell, for example, constant current, constant power, or a complex load cycle. Finally, we select the optimization criteria, for example, areal capacity or cell-level specific energy.

In the first case study, we report a simple scenario that closely resembles many academic electrode optimization studies in the literature. The optimization parameters are cathode AM% and porosity, with a fixed binder adhesion and AM mass loading per unit area of current collector (an arbitrary constraint that is removed in the final case study). A half-cell discharge is performed under a range of constant currents from 0.1C to 3C, between fixed cutoff voltages of 4.2 and 3.2 V, as defined by the open-circuit voltage curves provided in the parameter set reported by Chen et al.
21 Note that in this case, because mass loading is fixed and no cell normalization is performed, the theoretical capacity of each sample is the same. It is thus possible to define operating conditions relative to 1C (i.e., the current required to discharge the theoretical capacity in 1 h). We optimize the capacity per gram of Ni0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC) (Coulombic capacity), which negates the relevance of the overall cell geometry.

The results are not plotted due to an interesting degeneracy; for all currents, the optimal manufacturing parameters are the highest porosity and lowest AM% in the envelope permitted by the training data. This is because high porosity results in a lower tortuosity factor, and low AM% gives good conductivity due to high conductive binder content. The resulting optimal cathodes are the thickest of all the simulated scenarios, but the effect of longer transport pathways is outweighed by good transport coefficients in all cases. However, would these cells be commercially useful? Porous electrodes with high binder content and more porosity have lower theoretical energy density due to a low AM volume fraction. Importantly, this is not taken into account when the capacity per gram of AM is the optimization target. Case study 1 serves as a useful reminder that the impact of microstructure on rate performance cannot be usefully investigated when results are normalized against AM mass despite the common appearance of this type of analysis in the experimental literature. 22
,
23
,
24

To this end, for our second case study, we optimize the same parameters, but now under constant power discharge, with cell-level-specific energy as the optimization criteria. Constant power is a more industrially relevant condition (e.g., an electric vehicle driving at constant speed) and also more demanding than constant current since the current must be gradually increased to account for the decreasing cell voltage. Since power is being applied at the cell level, the length of the jelly roll determines the areal power density (assumed to be uniform over the jelly roll). The 4680 cell format is used for the normalization, and its dimensions are described in cell simulation. As described in optimization loop, in each iteration, the cathode (with a theoretical separator, current collector, and matched capacity anode) is fitted into the 4680 cell, from which the cell mass can be calculated. The results of this optimization for a range of powers are drastically different from case study 1, as shown in Figure 2 .

At low powers, the optimal manufacturing parameters are low porosity and high AM%, which is the opposite of case study 1. Given the fixed AM mass loading in this case study, these parameters result in a thin jelly roll, meaning a longer jelly roll can be wound inside the 4680 cell. Although the effective ionic transport parameters in the electrolyte and the electronic conductivity in the solid phase are all comparatively low, this has little impact during a low power discharge. It is worth noting that although the cell is thin, case study 1 implies that generally, a thicker, more porous electrode gives better transport properties for the fixed mass loading used in these cases. Conversely, at high powers, a porous microstructure with a high carbon domain volume fraction is optimal, as the transport processes become dominant. The resulting cells are thicker but give better specific energy, as a larger portion of the theoretical capacity can be accessed. The conclusions drawn after simple cell normalization are drastically different compared to those from case study 1, highlighting the importance of carefully selecting the optimization criteria. It is also worth noting that the behavior shown in these optimization runs indicates that the cathode, not the anode, is the limiting factor for specific energy. Were this not the case, the optimal parameters for all powers would be the lowest porosity and highest AM%, to give the thinnest possible cathode and, thus, allow the largest area of jelly roll to fit in the casing. Importantly, in this case study, the mass loading is still fixed, meaning that given an AM% and porosity, the thickness of the cathode is fixed.

In the third case study, we allowed the mass loading to vary and also included the binder adhesion parameter, giving 4 optimization parameters in total. We also changed the objective to maximizing energy density (rather than specific energy) to demonstrate the flexibility of the optimizations workflow. We expect to see thick, low-porosity, low-binder-fraction electrodes at low powers and the reverse at high powers. This intuition is based on the fact that thicker electrodes result in longer transport paths (problematic at high powers but not low powers), but a higher proportion of the cell mass is AM (not beneficial at high powers if most of the capacity cannot be accessed due to poor transport).

Since there are now four optimizations parameters, we can no longer easily plot the path through parameter space. Instead, in Figure 3 , we show a Ragone plot of power density vs. energy density. The solid lines show the performance of two particular cells across different powers. The blue line shows the performance of an “energy cell” that was optimized for energy density at 10 W, and the red line shows the performance of a “power cell” that was optimized for energy density at 200 W. Further details of these two cells and their manufacturing parameters are given in Table 1 . The black dashed line depicts the set of cells with the maximum attainable energy density at each power density when varying the four parameters of case study 3. The gray dashed line shows the cell performance from case study 1 (normalized to full cells in 4680 cell geometry), where highly porous electrodes with a lower AM mass fraction were preferred at all currents. The poor performance of this cell set at all powers illustrates the significance of cell-level normalization during optimization.

The performance curves of the energy cell and the power cell cross at a ca. 54 W discharge, which also roughly corresponds to the 1 h discharge isochrone. Although this means that in 1 h, both cells release ca. 54 Wh of energy before reaching their lower cutoff voltage, it is important to remember that due to their distinct low-rate capacities, these would represent different P-rates (i.e., power divided by nominal energy capacity) for the two cells. Nonetheless, at 54 W, these two cells would be superficially hard to distinguish, other than the energy cell being ca. 7% heavier.

The fact that no single set of parameters produces a cell that performs well for all powers clearly demonstrates the importance of bespoke cell design. Interestingly, however, the energy cell sits very close to the path of the optimal set below a discharge rate of ca. 50 W (equivalent to ca. 0.5 P). This shows that at low powers, high mass loading, low porosity, and high AM% lead to the best cell performance, as expected.

As previously discussed, we have selected a number of case studies to demonstrate the potential strengths of this cell design approach. However, numerous scenarios could easily be implemented due to the modular nature of the optimization loop. For example, the generative model could be trained to include a large number of parameters not in the scope of this study, including different chemistries or manufacturing processes. Separate models to generate the anode and separator microstructures could also be incorporated in order to co-optimize each component at the cell level. Graded electrodes with complex varying volume fractions as a function of depth could also be included.

Generative models can be a powerful tool for material design, allowing the relationships between manufacturing parameters, material microstructure, and component performance to be linked and explored. However, as shown in this work, careful consideration is necessary to ensure that the conclusions drawn from computational optimizations experiments are useful: synthetic data from the model must be statistically validated against held-back test data to ensure that trends are captured, the volume and batch size of microstructures must be large enough to give high-accuracy performance metrics, and the optimization target must be selected to reflect the needs of practical applications.

Case studies 2 and 3 demonstrate that when these criteria are met, our optimization workflow can yield valuable insights into lithium-ion cell design. Thousands of potential microstructures are generated and evaluated for each study, giving a quantitative optimal path through parameter space according to the intended cell use. This approach has the potential to both improve cell performance through bespoke design and rapidly accelerate the cell design process.
The detailed methodology behind these results, including model training, optimization loop, and property extraction, is elaborated in the following section.

In this study, we used the generative model developed by Polaron. 17 It offers high data efficiency, with a single representative cross-sectional image per sample providing sufficient information for training. This is a highly desirable feature in the field of materials science, as microstructural imaging can be a slow and expensive process. The generation of three-dimensional (3D) microstructural data can lead to GPU memory challenges; however, the implementation in Polaron’s platform requires an order of magnitude less memory than was required by SliceGAN. 25 This allows larger, more representative samples to be used during training, resulting in higher-resolution, more realistic generations. Finally, as demonstrated in the results section, large volumes are often required to ensure statistical representativity. Polaron’s design tool incorporates uniform information density to allow the synthesis of arbitrarily large volumes. 25

Figure 4 shows images from the training set, as well as those generated by the trained generative model, for a variety of manufacturing conditions. Reassuringly, the generated microstructures are visually very similar to their training data counterparts with the same manufacturing parameters. Notice that the arrangement of particles in the training data is different for every manufacturing condition, which is to be expected from the physics-based model that produced them. However, for the generated data, it is possible, though not enforced, to create the same general arrangement of particles in each scenario while freely varying the manufacturing parameters. This is possible due to disentanglement, which has been the subject of numerous studies that seek to control individual features of images made by generative models. 26
,
27

To quantify the validity of the generative model, real and fake microstructures are compared in terms of their volume fractions, surface areas, and tortuosity factors. Eight manufacturing parameter sets that were not in the training set were selected for this test: all combinations of 93 or 95 AM%, 35% or 45% porosity, and binder adhesion of 0.25 or 0.75. For each condition, metrics are obtained by averaging over 100 samples, each of size 2563 voxels. As shown in Figure 5 , this volume size is sufficient to ensure good microstructural representativity. The relative errors for volume fraction, surface area, and tortuosity factor are 1.3%, 2.1%, and 1.9% errors, respectively. Note that the tested samples have the manufacturing parameters furthest from the training data points, meaning they require the largest interpolation by the model. We thus expect them to give an upper bound for the error in the model, as we are testing the most challenging microstructures to reconstruct. This provides a convincing argument that the generative model can faithfully predict microstructural metrics of unseen samples to reasonable accuracy.

The trained generative model is used to create microstructural volumes given a particular manufacturing condition. It is important to note that the model can produce microstructures around four orders of magnitude faster than the physics-based manufacturing simulations used to make the training data (although the comparison is not trivial due to the different hardware used). For example, in this study, the simulations used to generate a small training dataset ran between 12 and 48 h on 60–160 nodes, each consisting of 2× Intel E5-2695 CPUs with 128 GB memory. 20 Generating equivalent size volumes with a trained generative model on an RTX6000 GPU with 48 GB memory requires less than 5 s. Further discussion on the number and size of volumes used in this study to achieve representativity is given in the subsequent section on property extraction.

Next, the performance of the generated volume must be evaluated. Full 3D electrochemical battery models are currently too computationally expensive for the purposes of this study and would hinder our ability to explore a wide range of optimizations cases. Instead, five properties are calculated from the microstructures using TauFactor 2, an open-source GPU-optimized Python package. 18 These metrics will be used to parameterize a pseudo-2D (P2D) cell model (see cell simulation for further details). The first two microstructural properties are the AM and pore volume fractions, which are simple to obtain through voxel counting. The third, active surface area, is also fast to extract by counting neighboring voxels of the relevant phases. Finally, effective pore-phase diffusivity and effective solid-phase electrical conductivity are evaluated using the TauFactor 2 transport solver. Importantly, this solver allows different transport properties to be specified in each voxel, which is necessary to calculate the effective conductivity of the carbon binder and AM phases combined.

These five properties are relied on to capture the effects of microstructure on cell-level performance in the model. However, now that the Polaron platform has made microstructural generation so cheap, the property extraction simulations have become the most expensive step in the optimizations loop. Some studies have explored the use of machine learning methods for the fast prediction of effective properties—however, the training and validation of such models would add significant complexity to the proposed workflow, including generation and effective property calculation for hundreds of training data samples. 28 This can prove to be more computationally expensive than simply evaluating tortuosity during an optimization loop. Such approaches are thus beyond the scope of this study. Instead, we perform convergence analysis, as shown for the tortuosity factor in Figure 5, to find the most computationally efficient strategy for calculating the tortuosity factor to within a given confidence.

Figure 5A shows how the distribution of the tortuosity factors of 200 samples evolves as you increase volume size, while Figure 5B highlights that the mean tortuosity factor of these distributions converges to within 1% of the true value (which is calculated from a batch of 200 microstructures of edge length 800 voxels) at volumes above 1283 voxels. This shows that as long as the volumes are larger than 1283 voxel cubes, the true mean can be recovered with sufficient sampling. The third plot (Figure 5C) shows how the standard deviation reduces as the volume size increases. These first three plots use a fixed batch size of 200. However, the lower standard deviation of properties calculated from volumes with larger edge length means that a smaller batch size can be used to obtain a metric with the same standard error in the mean
(Equation 1)
ϵ μ
=
σ l
n
,
where σl is the standard deviation of a property calculated from cubes of size l and n is the number of samples in the batch.
ϵ μ
can also be defined in terms of statistical confidence through the following equation, where in this case, we target with 95% confidence that the mean is within 1% of the true value. This gives
(Equation 2)
1.96
ϵ μ
μ
< 0 . 01 ,
where μ is the distribution mean. Substituting Equation 1 into Equation 2 and rearranging for n gives
(Equation 3)
n >
(
196
σ l
μ
)
2
.

We can thus plot the required batch size as a function of volume for these confidence criteria, as shown in Figure 5D. By multiplying the time taken for a simulation of a given size, shown in Figure 5E, by the number of samples required at that size, we determine the total time for each size to achieve the desired accuracy (Figure 5F). Here, we find that 39 cubes with edge length 128 voxels (15 s total solve time in TauFactor 2) is the optimal setting for this generator (cubes of sizes 64 and 96 were found to overestimate the value of tortuosity by more than 0.5%, as shown in Figure 5B). Similar tests were performed for the other five metrics, as shown in supplemental experimental procedures section A.

The simulation stage of the optimization loop provides the link between microstructure and performance. Extracted microstructural metrics are passed to the PyBaMM implementation of the P2D model, 19 which is used to characterize the discharge behavior of the cells. Since PyBaMM automatically calculates the surface area (using the volume fraction and the particle radius), we applied a “surface area factor” as a correction term to account for surface coverage by carbon binder domain (CBD) measured with TauFactor in the generated volumes. Apart from the microstructure-dependent parameters discussed in property extraction, a further 25 parameters are required for the P2D model. These are taken from Chen et al., who performed a detailed characterization of an LGM50 cell with a graphite-SiO x negative electrode and an NMC positive electrode 21 (see supplemental experimental procedures section B for the full list of parameters).

The voltage and current curves from a P2D simulation can be used to calculate a performance metric, such as the capacity under a constant current boundary condition. In this study, we largely focus on specific energy and energy density at the cell level, which normalize cell-level performance according to the cell’s mass and volume, respectively. Further discussion on why this choice is made can be found in the results section.

In the three case studies, each manufacturing condition results in an electrode of a particular thickness. The total thickness of the jelly roll is the sum of the thicknesses of a pair of negative electrodes on either side of their copper current collector, a pair of positive electrodes on either side of their aluminum current collector, and two separators. We chose to design the electrodes for a cylindrical 4680 cell geometry, as this provides a volume constraint into which the jelly roll must fit. Figure 6 shows the various dimensions of a 4680 cell taken from a cell tear-down study. 29 The length of jelly roll that fits the volume can easily be calculated by dividing the cross-sectional area of the cell (minus a 4 mm diameter gap in the core) by the jelly roll thickness.

It is worth noting that under this setup, each hypothesized cell resulting from a particular manufacturing parameter set will having a different theoretical capacity, and thus, the C-rate (i.e., current divided by nominal capacity) becomes a confusing descriptor within a single optimization run. Instead, cell-level currents (or powers) are applied, which we also believe to be more industrially relevant. For the equations used in experiments optimizing energy per volume, refer to supplemental experimental procedures section C.

It is important to note the following assumptions and limitations in the P2D model used for this work. Many of these can be mitigated with more complex, computationally expensive simulations, which will be the subject of future studies.
(1)
Contact losses between the electrodes and current collectors are omitted. If included, the resulting overpotential would reduce the energy density of the cell.
(2)
Thermal effects are not taken into account, which would become especially important at high rates.
(3)
Simple solid-state diffusion and a concentrated electrolyte model are used.
(4)
Currents and capacities are assumed to be uniform across the jelly roll.
(5)
Geometry is simplified, such as the lack of particle cracking, use of mean particle size, no swelling during lithiation, and voxel-based surface area.
(6)
No degradation mechanisms are accounted for, meaning that performance is only optimized for beginning-of-life performance.

The goal of the final module of the optimization loop is to select the manufacturing parameters to query potential candidates for new and improved microstructures. A naive approach to this would be to perform a grid or random search of the design space, where at each point, a microstructure is generated and evaluated. However, it is well established that this is an inefficient optimization strategy, as a significant amount of time is spent evaluating low-performance regions of the design space. Furthermore, the number of experiments to perform scales exponentially with the number of manufacturing parameters to be optimized. For this reason, the Polaron platform employs a form of Bayesian optimization, which uses a stochastic Gaussian process to select the best manufacturing parameter set to test by evaluating the benefit of exploration (searching new, untested regions of the parameter cube) vs. exploitation (searching around probable peaks in performance). 30 Bayesian optimization is well suited to this task for a number of reasons. First, while microstructure generation is very fast (
< 1
s per representative sample), the subsequent extraction of the tortuosity factor and conductivity takes ca. 2 min. If a 3D electrochemical model replaced the P2D model, this would further increase computational expense. It is thus important to choose data points to maximize information gain. For example, case study 3 explores four microstructural parameters, for which using a grid would require thousands of evaluations.
The lead contact is Steve Kench (steve@polaron.ai).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.
The codes used in this manuscript are available at https://github.com/stke9/Micro-Optimisation. All other generated data used are available from the authors upon request.

This work was supported by funding from the EPSRC Faraday Institution Multi-Scale Modeling project (https://faraday.ac.uk/; EP/S003053/1, grant number FIRG003 received by S.K.). The Titan Xp GPU used for this research was kindly donated by the Nvidia Corporation through their GPU Grant program to S.J.C. The authors would also like to thank the tldr group for their valuable comments. This paper describes objective technical results and analysis. Any subjective views or opinions that might be expressed in the paper do not necessarily represent the views of the United States (US) Department of Energy or the US government. This article has been authored by an employee of National Technology & Engineering Solutions of Sandia, LLC, under contract no. DE-NA0003525, with the US Department of Energy (DOE). The employee owns all right, title, and interest in and to the article and is solely responsible for its contents. The US government retains and the publisher, by accepting the article for publication, acknowledges that the US government retains a non-exclusive, paid-up, irrevocable, worldwide license to publish or reproduce the published form of this article or allow others to do so, for US government purposes. The DOE will provide public access to these results of federally sponsored research in accordance with the DOE Public Access Plan (https://www.energy.gov/downloads/doe-public-access-plan).

S.K. and S.J.C. conceptualized the project. S.K. trained the models, performed the statistical analysis, and drafted the manuscript. I.S., A.D., S.A.R., and F.B.P. contributed to the development of the concepts presented in all sections of this work, helped with data interpretation, and made substantial revisions and edits to all sections of the manuscript.
S.K., I.S., and S.J.C. are all founders and shareholders of Polaron (www.polaron.ai), whose software was used for the microstructural optimization.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matt.2024.08.014.
